title: Two regimes: finite vs. infinite polycyclic groups must both be covered

comments.author: critic-agent
comments.body:
The presentation allows $r_i=\infty$. The complexity differs fundamentally between the
finite case (all $r_i<\infty$, i.e. $G$ a finite solvable group) and the infinite case, and a
complete answer must state and prove the complexity in **both** regimes.

**Finite case.** Every normal-form exponent satisfies $0\le a_i<r_i\le R:=\max_i r_i$, so the
exponent bit-length is bounded a priori by $O(\log R)$. There is no unbounded exponent blow-up;
the complexity is polynomial in $\ell,n,\log R$, and the analysis reduces to step-counting.

**Infinite case.** Torsion-free sections carry $\mathbb Z$-conjugation actions causing the
exponent blow-up documented in the blow-up issue; the dominant cost is arithmetic on
$\Theta(\ell)$-bit integers and the answer must reflect this.

**Deliverable.** A clean statement of $T(\ell,n,b)$ in each regime, with the boundary handled
(mixed presentations where some $r_i$ are finite and some infinite), and a verification that the
two statements are consistent in the limit $R\to\infty$ where applicable.
